userStories.title: Migrate runtime from Node + tsgo to Bun
userStories.description: Replace the current build+run pipeline (tsgo compile then node execute) with Bun, which compiles and runs TypeScript natively. This eliminates the separate build step, simplifies the dev workflow, and speeds up execution. Bun is a drop-in Node replacement that supports ESM, TypeScript, and all Node APIs used by agent-cli (child_process, fs, path, etc.).
acceptanceCriteria: Replace npm scripts: dev/start use 'bun run src/index.ts' instead of tsgo build + node dist/index.js
Remove or demote tsgo and tsc build scripts (keep as fallback with build:old or build:tsc)
Verify all Node built-in modules used (child_process, fs, path, readline, etc.) work under Bun
Verify chalk and commander work under Bun
Update CLAUDE.md Build & Run section to reflect Bun as the primary runtime
npm run dev still works as the main development command
Build passes and agent-cli runs correctly with Bun
userStories.notes: tsgo is only a compiler (TS -> JS), not a runtime. Bun handles both compilation and execution natively, removing the build step entirely.

userStories.title: Fix stale branch detection to auto-update branchName instead of stopping
userStories.description: When the stale branch detection (US-010) finds that the branch in prd.json no longer exists, it currently clears branchName to empty string and stops the loop with an error — forcing the user to manually edit prd.json. Instead, it should detect the current git branch (git branch --show-current) and automatically update prd.json with it. If the current branch exists, continue the loop normally. If not on a branch (detached HEAD), only then stop with an error.
acceptanceCriteria: When branchName in prd.json no longer exists, detect current git branch via git branch --show-current
Auto-update prd.json branchName with the current branch instead of clearing it to empty string
Log a warning that the old branch was stale and the new branch was set
Continue the loop normally after updating — do not stop
If in detached HEAD state (no current branch), stop with an error as before
Archive the previous run before switching to the new branch
Build passes with npm run build
userStories.notes: Current behavior clears branchName and crashes. Found when switching from feat/new_tools (deleted) to feat/bun — had to manually edit prd.json. Depends on US-010 (stale branch detection).

userStories.title: Remove dead .last-branch mechanism from archiver
userStories.description: The .last-branch file is intended to detect branch changes between runs and trigger archiving, but it's broken by design: initialize() writes the current branch to .last-branch BEFORE checkAndArchive() reads it, so they always match and archiving on branch change never fires. Since stale branch detection (US-020) already handles the real-world case of switching branches, .last-branch is dead code. Remove the .last-branch file, the readLastBranch/writeLastBranch methods, and simplify checkAndArchive accordingly.
acceptanceCriteria: Delete the .last-branch file from the repo
Remove readLastBranch and writeLastBranch methods from Archiver
Simplify checkAndArchive to remove the branch comparison logic
Remove the lastBranchPath property and LAST_BRANCH_FILE constant
Remove the .last-branch write from initialize()
iterator.ts no longer references .last-branch behavior
Add .last-branch to .gitignore
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: The archiver still archives via handleStaleBranch() in iterator.ts, so removing .last-branch doesn't break the archiving use case. The branch comparison in checkAndArchive was dead logic.

userStories.title: Translate README.md to English
userStories.description: The current README.md is partially or fully in Portuguese. Translate all content to English so the project is accessible to a broader audience.
acceptanceCriteria: All text in README.md is in English
Technical terms, code examples, and project structure remain accurate
No content is lost or changed in meaning during translation
npm run build passes

userStories.title: Create architecture documentation with ASCII art and Mermaid diagrams
userStories.description: Generate visual architecture docs for agent-cli. Create an ASCII art diagram in .tmp/architecture.md showing the main loop flow, module relationships, and data flow between components. Also create .tmp/architecture-mermaid.md with Mermaid versions of the same diagrams. Include suggestions for other useful views (e.g., session lifecycle, archiving flow, PRD state machine) as separate sections or files.
acceptanceCriteria: .tmp/architecture.md contains ASCII art diagrams of the core loop and module relationships
.tmp/architecture-mermaid.md contains equivalent Mermaid diagrams
Diagrams cover: main loop flow (iterator), tool spawning, PRD state transitions, archiving flow, session management
Additional suggested views are documented (e.g., error handling flow, git status tracking)
.tmp directory is gitignored and does not appear in version control
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: The .tmp/ directory is for local-only documentation that helps developers visualize the system. Not meant to be committed.

userStories.title: Add global .watch.json config for monitored directories
userStories.description: Create a global config file at ~/.agent-cli/.watch.json that lists directories to monitor. This allows the monitor subcommand (US-025) to know which projects to track. Provide a way to add/remove directories via CLI flags.
acceptanceCriteria: Global config stored at ~/.agent-cli/.watch.json with { directories: string[] } format
~/.agent-cli/ directory is auto-created if it doesn't exist
Add directory via --add <path> flag (resolves to absolute path)
Remove directory via --remove <path> flag
Add types WatchConfig and ProjectStatus to types.ts
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: This is the data foundation for the monitor dashboard. Keep it simple — just a JSON array of absolute paths. BullMQ will replace file-based state later.

userStories.title: Implement agent-cli monitor subcommand with live table
userStories.description: Add a `monitor` subcommand that reads .watch.json and polls each directory every 2 seconds, rendering a live-updating table showing project name, branch, iteration progress, story progress, status, cost, and last activity. Uses only existing dependencies (chalk + ANSI escape codes) — no new deps. Status detection: running (session exists, timestamp <30s), idle (session exists, stale), done (no session, all stories pass), stopped (no session, incomplete).
acceptanceCriteria: New file src/core/monitor.ts with Monitor class
register monitor subcommand in src/index.ts via commander
Table displays: PROJECT, BRANCH, ITER, STORIES, STATUS, COST, LAST ACTIVITY
Table refreshes in-place every 2s using ANSI escape codes
Status detection works: running, idle, done, stopped
Graceful exit on Ctrl+C with process signal handling
Shows empty state message when no directories are configured
No new dependencies — uses chalk only
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Depends on US-024 for .watch.json config. Monitor runs in its own terminal — doesn't interfere with running agent-cli instances. Designed so the data source can be swapped to BullMQ later.

userStories.title: Detect branch change via current git branch instead of .last-branch
userStories.description: Currently archiving only triggers when the branch in prd.json no longer exists (deleted). But if the user simply switches branches with git checkout, the old branch still exists and nothing detects the change. Fix: before starting the loop, compare prd.json branchName with the current git branch (git branch --show-current). If they differ, trigger archiving and update prd.json — even if the old branch still exists.
acceptanceCriteria: Compare prd.json branchName with git branch --show-current before the loop starts
If they differ, archive the old run and update prd.json with the current branch
Copy incomplete stories from old prd.json to new prd.json (US-027 dependency)
Log a warning that branch changed and archiving is happening
Continue the loop normally after updating
Works even when the old branch still exists locally
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Replaces the broken .last-branch detection. Depends on US-027 for story migration during archiving.

userStories.title: Migrate incomplete stories to new prd.json when archiving on branch change
userStories.description: When archiving triggers due to a branch change, the current behavior copies the entire old prd.json to the archive and starts fresh. This loses incomplete stories. Instead: copy incomplete stories (passes: false) from the old prd.json into the new prd.json, renumbering their priorities if needed. Completed stories stay only in the archive as a record of what was done.
acceptanceCriteria: Before archiving, identify stories with passes: false in the current prd.json
After archiving, write a new prd.json that includes only the incomplete stories
Incomplete stories keep their id, title, description, acceptanceCriteria, and notes
Reset passes to false on migrated stories
Completed stories are NOT copied to the new prd.json — they live in the archive
Update branchName in the new prd.json to the current branch
Log how many stories were migrated vs archived
If all stories were complete, new prd.json can be empty or minimal
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: This ensures no work is lost when switching branches. The archive preserves the full history including completed stories.

userStories.title: Fix monitor screen flickering with alternate screen buffer
userStories.description: The monitor subcommand flickers every 2 seconds because it uses ANSI clearScreen (\x1b[2J) which erases and redraws the entire terminal. Fix by using an alternate screen buffer (\x1b[?1049h to enter, \x1b[?1049l to exit) — the same technique used by htop, vim, and less. This draws to an invisible buffer so there's no visible flash. No new dependencies needed.
acceptanceCriteria: Enter alternate screen buffer on monitor start (\x1b[?1049h)
Exit alternate screen buffer on monitor stop and Ctrl+C (\x1b[?1049l)
Remove ANSI.clearScreen from the render cycle — use cursor movement only
No visible flickering during refresh
Graceful exit restores the original terminal content
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Two-line fix in monitor.ts. No TUI library needed.

userStories.title: Fix monitor stacking lines instead of overwriting
userStories.description: The monitor was appending new content every refresh cycle instead of overwriting in place. The root cause: cursor-up movement counted lines including ANSI escape codes from chalk, so the cursor moved the wrong number of lines. Fix: in alternate screen buffer, always move cursor to home position (\x1b[H) and erase down before each render, instead of counting lines to move up. Remove the renderedLines tracking property.
acceptanceCriteria: render() uses cursorHome + eraseDown instead of cursorUp by line count
Remove renderedLines property from Monitor class
No line stacking or duplication during refresh
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Fixed by replacing line-counting cursor movement with cursorHome (\x1b[H) which is reliable in alternate screen buffer.

userStories.title: Rewrite monitor with Ink TUI for richer interaction
userStories.description: Replace the current ANSI-based monitor with an Ink (React for CLI) TUI. Ink provides component-based rendering, automatic layout, keyboard input handling, and eliminates manual ANSI escape code management. The new TUI should keep the same project table but add interactive features: scroll through projects with arrow keys, expand a project to see story-level detail, toggle between views (table vs detail), and show real-time logs for a selected project.
acceptanceCriteria: Install ink and react as dependencies (ink, react, @types/react)
Replace monitor.ts ANSI rendering with Ink components
Project table component shows same columns as current: PROJECT, BRANCH, ITER, STORIES, STATUS, COST, LAST ACTIVITY
Keyboard navigation: arrow keys to select project, Enter to expand detail view
Detail view shows individual stories with status (pass/fail) for selected project
Press 'q' or Escape to exit, 't' to toggle back to table view
Alternate screen buffer still used for clean exit
Data polling logic (2s interval, session/prd reading) reused from current implementation
No flickering or stacking issues
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Ink is ~50KB, React-like components for CLI. Current polling logic in collectProjectStatus() should be reused as a hook or service. This replaces US-028/029's manual ANSI approach with a proper component model.

userStories.title: Create Dockerfile for agent runtime sandbox
userStories.description: Create a Dockerfile that builds a minimal agent runtime image with Bun, Claude Code CLI, and git. The image is used to run agents in an isolated container where only the project directory is accessible. This provides OS-level sandboxing that prevents agents from accessing or modifying files outside their project, regardless of what commands they try to execute.
acceptanceCriteria: Dockerfile at project root with Bun, git, and Claude Code CLI
Image runs as non-root user (no sudo access)
Multi-stage build to keep image small
Verify image builds: docker build -t agent-cli-runner .
Container can run claude --version successfully
.dockerignore excludes node_modules, .git, archive, .tmp
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Foundation for US-032. The image should be lightweight — only what's needed to run the agent. No dev dependencies.

userStories.title: Add --sandbox flag and Docker spawn mode to tool-runner
userStories.description: Add a --sandbox CLI flag that makes tool-runner spawn the AI agent inside a Docker container instead of directly on the host. When sandboxed: mount only the project directory as read-write, use --rm for ephemeral containers, set resource limits (memory, CPU), and optionally restrict network. The rest of the agent-cli orchestration (iterator, PRD, archiving) continues to run on the host — only the agent tool runs inside the container.
acceptanceCriteria: Add --sandbox flag to CLI (src/index.ts)
Add SandboxConfig type to types.ts with image, memory, cpu options
tool-runner detects sandbox mode and uses docker run instead of direct spawn
Container mounts only the project directory (-v $projectDir:/workspace)
Container uses --rm flag (ephemeral, removed after iteration)
Container runs as non-root with --user flag
Stdout/stderr streaming still works for completion signal detection
Prompt file piped to container stdin works correctly
Add config defaults for Docker image name and resource limits
Without --sandbox, behavior is unchanged (backward compatible)
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Depends on US-031 for the Docker image. The host-side orchestration (iterator, PRD management, archiving) runs outside the container — only the agent tool is sandboxed.

userStories.title: Add scoped permission mode as default, keep dangerous mode as fallback option
userStories.description: The current tool config in config.ts uses --dangerously-skip-permissions which bypasses all of Claude Code's safety checks. Add a scoped permission mode as the DEFAULT that uses --allowedTools with an allowlist of permitted operations. Keep --dangerously-skip-permissions as a fallback option accessible via a flag (e.g. --yolo or --unsafe). Do NOT remove the dangerous option — the user wants to keep it available for trusted/fast iteration scenarios. The scoped mode uses .claude/settings.json allowlist: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep, Bash(npm *), Bash(git *), Bash(bun *), Bash(node *), Bash(ls *), Bash(rm *) — everything else is blocked.
acceptanceCriteria: Add --permission-mode flag to CLI with options: 'scoped' (default) and 'yolo' (keeps --dangerously-skip-permissions)
Scoped mode uses --allowedTools instead of --dangerously-skip-permissions
Allowlist: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep, Bash(npm *), Bash(git *), Bash(bun *), Bash(node *), Bash(ls *), Bash(rm *)
Yolo mode keeps existing behavior with --dangerously-skip-permissions (no changes)
agent-cli generates or copies the settings.json into the project dir before spawning agent in scoped mode
Document both modes and when to use each
Test that agent can still complete normal tasks in scoped mode (read, write, build, test, git, rm files)
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: IMPORTANT: Do NOT remove --dangerously-skip-permissions. Keep it as 'yolo' mode for trusted scenarios. The scoped mode is the safer default. Works with US-032 Docker for defense in depth — even with rm in allowlist, Docker limits filesystem reach.

userStories.title: Add prompt-level safety constraints to agent-cli.md
userStories.description: Strengthen the agent-cli.md prompt file with explicit safety rules that the agent must follow. These are soft constraints (prompt-level) that complement the hard constraints from Docker (US-032) and permission config (US-033). Include rules about: never modifying files outside the project directory, never running destructive shell commands, never accessing credentials or secrets, never installing global packages, and always validating file paths before operations.
acceptanceCriteria: Add safety section to agent-cli.md with explicit rules
Rules cover: filesystem boundaries, destructive commands, credentials, global installs, network requests
Include examples of forbidden patterns (rm -rf /, sudo, curl | bash, etc.)
Rules are concise and unambiguous — no room for interpretation
Agent can still perform all legitimate development tasks
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Softest layer of defense but still valuable — prevents accidental damage even in non-sandboxed environments. Works with US-032 and US-033 for defense in depth.

userStories.title: Write human-readable agent logs for monitor and tmux tail
userStories.description: The .agent-output.log file currently contains raw stream-json events because tool-runner writes raw chunks to the log file. Fix: tool-runner must write PARSED, human-readable output to .agent-output.log — reuse the same format that createStreamEventHandler() already produces for process.stdout (tool names with args, text output, iteration markers). This log file is the data source for both the monitor TUI and the tmux integration (US-036).
acceptanceCriteria: tool-runner writes human-readable output to .agent-output.log (not raw stream-json). Reuse the parsing from createStreamEventHandler() — same format shown on stdout: 'Using: Read /path/to/file', text content, iteration headers, cost/duration summaries
Write to log file in real-time as events are parsed (streaming, not buffered until end)
.agent-output.log is added to .gitignore
No new dependencies required
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Root cause: line 291 in tool-runner.ts does logStream.write(chunk) with raw stream-json data. The fix is to write the parsed/formatted output to the log instead — createStreamEventHandler() already produces human-readable output via process.stdout.write() and chalk formatting. The simplest approach: make handleStreamEvent return the formatted strings and write those to the log file. For non-stream-json tools (amp), the raw output is already human-readable so no change needed.

userStories.title: Add tmux split-pane log viewer to monitor TUI
userStories.description: Integrate tmux into the monitor TUI so that pressing 'l' on a selected project opens a tmux split pane running `tail -f .agent-output.log` for that project. This allows viewing real-time agent output for multiple projects simultaneously — each in its own tmux pane. The monitor table stays in the main pane. tmux is optional: if not installed, the monitor shows a message suggesting tmux installation and falls back to the Ink-based logs view (last N lines refreshed every 2s).
acceptanceCriteria: Detect if running inside tmux (check TERM and TMUX env vars) and if tmux binary is available
Press 'l' on a selected project: if tmux available, open a split pane with `tail -f <project>/.agent-output.log`
Support multiple split panes — pressing 'l' on another project opens another split
Press 'L' (shift+l) to close all log panes and return to single-pane monitor
Each log pane has a header/border showing the project name
If tmux not available, fall back to Ink-based inline log view (tail last 50 lines, refresh every 2s)
Monitor must be started inside a tmux session for split-pane to work — detect and warn if not
Graceful cleanup: closing the monitor closes all spawned tail processes
No new dependencies required — tmux is a system tool
npm run build passes
userStories.notes: Depends on US-035 (human-readable log file). Approach: monitor detects tmux availability at startup. When 'l' is pressed, runs `tmux split-pane -h 'tail -f <path>/.agent-output.log'` via child_process.execSync. The monitor itself must run inside tmux for split-pane to work. If not inside tmux, show warning and fall back to Ink inline view. tmux is NOT a hard dependency — monitor works without it, just without live split panes.
